sentences.text: Yeah, we were taken off the train, and, of course, my uncle didn't know anything about this.
And he was very upset when he found out, obviously, for obvious reasons.
The reason he had-- we left behind-- that we hadn't been chosen to go on that train is because he had-- that same evening he had gone to whoever.
I don't think-- don't think I can say to the commandant.
He had got a deferment, and he-- he understood it and encompassed the whole family.
You know.
He didn't know that his son and-- and me were about to go on the train, but this is how the Germans functioned.
They didn't care.
You know.
And neither did-- did everybody else.
The Dutch didn't care, and some of the Jewish police didn't care, either.
As long as they could save their own skin, they didn't care.

One famous one-- which I really had forgotten about, but somebody reminded me of it recently-- was there was a German Jew living in Holland called Friedrich Weinreb who was negotiating with the Germans for money to get so and so many people off transports and kept in Holland.
OK.
Now, of course, it's very controversial.
He's a very controversial person, and people said he sold, you know, he sold the Jews.
And he made money out of it, and he was saved.
But anyway, at the time, there was called a Weinreb list.
OK.
He could have been put on there because my uncle had money outside, you know, with-- with friends, but then he decided he didn't like that.
You know.
He wasn't that happy with Weinreb, and-- and I think he knew him vaguely.
And then he hit upon the idea, we had these Red Cross letters, those which we had gotten.
And he went and said, look.
Those two children are actually English citizens, because their mother is living in England.
And she's probably English by now, and-- and they are English citizens.
OK.
And this actually-- I don't know whether they bought the whole story, but the fact-- there was proof that she was living in England.
There was no question about that.
That is-- they made up the list.
The Jews, themselves, made up the list, by the way.
The Germans demanded, I don't know 1,000, whatever it was, 1,500, and they made up the list.
I knew about it at the time.
I heard about the Weinreb list.
OK.
And I heard about it, and then I remember later on when it-- sort of, like, almost lists, all sort of lists, you know, different lists.
I don't remember their names, but they all in the end they went.
You know, they didn't help.
You-- you were deferred, and then when the Germans decided the time had come, they took you also.
And there was nobody else left usually, or less people left.
So Weinreb, he was well-known.
I knew about him because a fella-- a couple of years ago somebody called.
I had written some articles in the local Jewish papers here about Westerbork and about Bergen-Belsen and about myself, and some older gentleman called me.
He said he would like to see me.
I said, what do you want to see me for?
He said, well, I want to discuss your articles.
I said, well, they're not really that-- I mean, they're OK, but there's nothing much to discuss.
He said, well, I would like to see you.
And when I walk into his apartment, he says, what do you think of Friedrich Weinreb?
So I was taken aback because I-- it was like a dim memory to me.
It was never that important to me, you know.
So I said, I don't know, which probably was a good answer because it turns out that his-- I think his-- his daughter married Weinreb's son.
Or his son married Weinreb's daughter, something like that.
He was related through marriage, you know, and he told me about him, that after the war Weinreb was imprisoned together with Germans.
He was put in prison together with German-- German SS and things like that.
Then, of course, he was let go because they didn't want to keep him.
Even though he was-- some people accused him of all sort of things, he was let go because he was Jewish, and he immigrated to Indonesia.
He was a professor, and he became a professor in Indonesia.
And I think he died there, but his family lives in-- in-- in Belgium.

And that's why I remembered Weinreb all of a sudden, even though I knew about him, and he was-- basically it was a sort of-- more than anybody else it was a whole-- it was a whole big thing about him because he was accused of dealing with the German authorities, you know, and selling Jews.
But he claims just the opposite, that he was trying to save Jews, and it was never resolved really.
And he-- this gentleman made me-- he wrote books in German, huge books like this.
And I don't read German that well, you know, so I started to read.
And then I decided to-- you know, they were very self-serving books that tried to explain his-- his point of view.
And had they been in English, I probably would have had read them, but being in German, I decided you know, I didn't-- I couldn't be bothered.
So I brought them back to him, and-- but I remember then-- then I also asked somebody else about him, Professor [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know if anybody ever heard of him.
He's one of the foremost authorities on the-- And he knew about him also, so he knows-- knows more about Eastern European people like [INAUDIBLE] He knows more about than what he knew about him.
And he also said, look.
It's difficult to judge from where we are today whether they were right or wrong what they did.
But anyway, to go back to my story, we-- so he was called to go to-- on a train, which I didn't know at the time was going to Sobibor, together with my aunt and-- and my cousin in 1942.
This must have been in June, July.
I think I have the exact date.
I gave you the date, I think, when he died.
And--
'43, Yeah, not '42, I'm sorry.
Did I say '42?
Yeah, '43, it must be '43.
I think it was June or July '43, and they were killed in Sobibor.
OK.
But he had made arrangements for us to stay in an orphanage.
There was an orphanage.
Westerbork was run in a way-- if you know the story about Thereisenstadt, Terezin, it-- it was in a way similar.
Even though it was a transit camp, Theresienstadt also was.
There were all sort of things going on inside the camp.
You know, it was-- For instance, the orphanage was run by a gentleman called Yehoshua Berenbaum with his wife, who had been in Westerbork since 1939.
Westerbork had been established by the Dutch.
They used it for German refugees who came after the Kristallnacht.
And they didn't know what to do with them, so in the end, they put them in Westerbork, which was way out, like I said, in-- like I said, in [? left ?] field.
And people didn't know where it was, and that's where they kept them.
But they ran their own camp.
In those days, they ran the camp themselves.
There was a Dutch police commandant in charge, but there was no barbed wire around it.
They could leave, if they-- but they couldn't.
They had nowhere to go, so they stayed there.
And he was one of those people who had been there from the very beginning.
You know, first of all, the poor lady, Miep, who had to supply them, had to find food for eight people who were living illegally.
Secondly, if you were caught, everybody was caught.
So most families split up.
You know, they had the parents in one place and the children in another place, or even-- they split off even more than that.
First of all, there were two-- two reasons.
Number one, it was easier for the people who were hiding them, and also for this other reason that if somebody got caught, maybe somebody else would survive.
So for this reason, you had the orphans.
You know, many of them were-- were not originally orphans.
But their parents had been caught, or they had been caught.
And-- and they had nobody to stay with.
OK, in Westerbork, all together, we were privileged that we were allowed to receive parcels from the outside.
And the-- so Dutch people would send us parcels.
I-- I know my sister and myself got a lot of parcels from friends, who-- some of my uncles were sending those parcels, and they, of course, went to the orphanage, like everybody else's.
So we were fairly well, as far as food was concerned, fairly well taken care of.
OK.
He wouldn't-- he wouldn't give in, you know, so every week some children went, which was very sad.
You know.
And-- and we were one of the few who weren't called for quite a while.
And when I got into the office, there must have been about 50, 60 kids there, one side, the girls, and one side, boys.
And there was also a small room for the little babies.
By the end of '43, there may have been 10, 15 left, aside from the Berenbaums themselves.
I'll tell you some-- you know, some of the things which went on in Westerbork.
For instance, I think this was harassment by the commandant, you know.

The commandant, when Berenbaum used to haunt him every week, I think he got fed up with him, you know, so one week he decided that we had to go in quarantine because there was an outbreak of some disease.
I don't know what it was, what they claimed it was.
I think that they said it was probably polio or something like that.
You know.
I don't think anybody was sick, so they put barbed wire around-- just around our-- our orphanage.
And then people used to communicate through the barbed wire, so they used to put another barbed wire around the orphanage.
So we were really isolated.
I think this was more or less a form of-- of telling Berenbaum to lay off, you know, to-- to-- because he couldn't go out anymore.
We couldn't go out of the barracks.
We were just in there, and-- and-- but it wasn't like awful.
We couldn't move out, and he couldn't get out, either.
And he couldn't go out and-- and go to [INAUDIBLE] because he just couldn't go out and, you know, things like that.
And then we had a sort of distant relative who was a cousin of my father, I think she was.
Her name was Johanna Hamburger.
That was her husband's name, Hamburger.
I think she might have been called Spiegel.
The German Jews who had been in the camp in 1939 didn't live in the big barracks like we did.
They had-- originally, they had like little apartments, small, but maybe one or two rooms.
And then when it became the transit camp in-- it actually only became a transit camp in 1942, by the way.
You know, the Germans took it over.
They were sort of squeezed a little bit, but they still had a little room of their own.
So it was easier to have parcels sent to-- to them than to the big barracks, and then she would bring them to us.
I remember.
I still see that picture, too, in front of my eyes where she would try to get the parcels to the orphanage when we were in isolation.

She used to take a-- a long piece of wood, a flat piece of wood, put the parcel on the end of that piece of wood, and push the wood underneath both barbed wire fences to-- to-- to give us those-- those parcels.
This was one of the things I remember, you know, and then I got sick.
I used to-- I had asthma, bronchitis.
And I got-- I don't know.
I may have had a small case of measles.
I don't know what it was.
I got sick, where I had to go to the hospital and-- they thought-- because they thought I had-- there was an outbreak of scarlet fever, not in the orphanage, in the camp.
And they thought that maybe I had scarlet fever, you know, so they took me to the hospital.
And I remember Berenbaum coming at night and saying, how do you feel?
I said, oh, I feel OK.
He said, well, I want you to come out because I don't like to leave you here because if you stay here, if there's a transport, they're gonna take you, which they did.
You know, very often they took people from the hospital and put them on the transport.
Sometimes people thought being in the hospital would-- would save them, but it didn't.
You know, it depends on-- on what the program was for that week.
You know, one week, it may have saved you.
The next week, they may have decided we gonna take all the sick people, all the old people.
I remember they used to put the old people, infirm, on those carts and wheel them to the train, you know, to the work camp, back to the east.
So-- I-- I-- I think I left that same evening or next day.
He took me out, you know, because he was afraid that if I would stay there, you know, that I would disappear.
Mostly dry goods, you know, not bread.
You couldn't send bread, but bread we got.
Maybe cookies, marmalade, candies probably, yeah, I imagine.
You know.
And probably canned-- you know, those-- you had some canned goods already, whatever they could get.
You know, Whatever was available, they would send, whatever they thought would be useful to us.
Yeah, yeah.
These were people.
One of the people who actively did this and was in [INAUDIBLE] actually at the time, I didn't know was working for the underground.
This was part of her-- her job, really, to-- to-- amongst other things, to-- to send supplies to people in prisons, you know, try to supply them.
This-- she didn't do this directly.
This was done through various channels in order for the Germans not to be able to trace anybody because one way for the Germans to trace people was to find out who sent those parcels.
You know.
It was done all in-- in-- you know, indirectly, but she was responsible for that most of the time.
And other people, too, got parcels.
We were not the only ones, but we got a lot.
I remember, it was tremendous.
I mean, it-- it was-- I remember that at one time I was embarrassed.
I mean, every day there was two of these parcels came in our name.
You know, luckily, we were living in the orphanage, so we-- you know, it's-- Berenbaum decided from the beginning everything should be shared, which was right.
It was obviously the correct thing to do.
He said, well, do you want to keep a few candies for yourself?
I said, no.
I mean, I-- you know, let's-- let's everybody.
So it was OK, but it was-- I remember this lady at the time came nearly every day to-- to-- to push parcels under the wire.
We were the only ones who got that many.
You know.
So then what happened when you came back out of the hospital?
I was back in the orphanage, and the orphanage was more or less a life of-- that tried to be normal.
You had some sort of schooling there, teaching.
There were a lot of teachers in-- in the camp.
There was a famous author-- children's author called Clara Asscher-Pinkhof, her name was.
I still can see her.
In my mind, I can still see her.
You know.
She was very good with little kids.
She didn't teach us, but she was very good with little kids.
And in Westerbork, also, at one time or another, there were very famous people there.
You know, the Dutch Jews were very integrated.
For instance, the orchestra, the concert orchestra, all the violinists, I think to the very last one, were Jewish.
So at one time, they picked them all up and brought them to Westerbork.
Then the Germans asked them to give a concert for them, you know, so they gave a concert for the Germans.
And they probably thought that if they gave concerts for the Germans, they would be deferred.
You know, they were deferred for maybe a week or two.
And unfortunately, I think if I remember rightly reading afterwards the story of Westerbork, that I think one survived of the whole-- of this whole group of violinists.
And then-- there was-- I don't remember his name-- there was a famous Jewish boxer who was well known, Dutch Jewish.
And he was bought into the camp, so he started organizing, like, exercising and-- and teaching young men if they want to box.
He was to-- you know, so they used to have boxing exhibitions for the Germans.
You know.
This-- this was a crazy situation when you think about it, you know.
This was a camp where-- where next week you might be dead, but the Germans came and watched you an exhibition of boxing.
Or they had a concert, or they had plays.
You know, they-- they had plays.
People were performing.
They had people who were-- who were actors, and they performed and-- and-- and all these kind of things.
Of course, they also performed for the inmates, you know, but the Germans had-- had them perform for them.
And especially around Christmas time, you know, they could do that kind of thing, or any holiday.
But we, as I said, they tried to teach us to be good Jews in-- in the orphanage, and, of course, it was religious.
And-- and-- and we had regular meal times.
We had to pray before and after the meal, and, like I said, to try to have sort of classes within the orphanage.
And then we used to walk around the camp, look for friends and visit friends, and-- and it was confined.
You couldn't go out.
It was-- it was thousands upon thousands of people in a very small area.
Difficult to-- it was maybe-- the whole camp might have been the size of a big football field, maybe a little bit bigger, but there were 20,000 or 30,000 people at the time there.
You know, so that's how confined it was.
You know, I mean, there was like a mass of people.
And, of course, the-- the saddest thing was that every-- every-- nearly every week there was-- sometimes, there was a break.
At one time, there was a break of a month or so, you know, for-- for whatever reason.
It was more technical.
It wasn't that they didn't want to send the Jews away, there were other reasons for it, you know, within-- within the German system.
No, we didn't.
What-- what-- what was happening to the people once they left, we didn't know, because we were too far away.
You know, you hear stories-- stories of people living in the Warsaw ghetto who were taken to Treblinka, and they escaped and came back and warned the people in the ghetto.
But that was close by.
OK.

People who were taken to Auschwitz from Holland had no way of-- of-- even if they weren't gassed right away, If they-- there was no way of-- even if they did escape, there was no way of making it back to Holland.
I don't know that anybody did escape, any Dutch Jews.
Maybe, maybe they didn't.
I don't know, but I mean, it was-- this was in Poland.
There was all of Germany in between, you know, so there wasn't a way of-- of, you know, finding out what it was all about.
It was just a name.
Auschwitz was a name.
What people were afraid of were, like I told you, Mauthausen.
That was the fear.
So they weren't sent to Mauthausen.
They were sent to Auschwitz, which was a labor camp, supposedly.
OK.
Then, of course, more people came in from the outside, and they brought the news.
OK.
That's how you knew what was happening.
That was your best-- usually the best sort of information, you know, people who knew people who came to the camp.
Nine months, about, you know.
After about nine months, they-- I can give you the exact date when we left.
It was January 11, 1944.
It was after nine months in Westerbork, and there were very few people left in the orphanage.
We were called to go on a train to Bergen-Belsen.
You know, the immigration to Palestine was very restricted, but they had got certificates to immigrate to Palestine, valid certificates.
But they got them too late.
They-- they couldn't get to Palestine.
I remember them well because we were-- at the time on that train, there were very few children.
I don't know why.
Later on, families came.
But at that time-- and we were sitting on the train with some of these people, and they sort of tried to watch us a little bit, take care of us.
And it took about two days to get to Bergen-Belsen.
Even though it wasn't that far, it took about two days.

When we got to Bergen-Belsen, the reception was sort of, you know, very scary because we arrived on the platform in a town called Celle, which is a sort of medieval town, which is I don't know how far, not that far, but far enough.
That's how far the train went, and when you got to the platform, you had to-- the German-- you only had to-- you didn't have no longer any Dutch police.
Even though the Dutch police weren't any better, you felt better about it because they were Dutch, or-- or you had the Jewish police in Westerbork.
There to meet us were just SS, you know, just the German guards, and we had to get off the train.
They made us, you know, stand in columns, and we started to walk into what's Bergen-Belsen.
OK.
They had some trucks, but they put-- some things-- we were carrying things.
They put it on there, and they put some-- some of the few women with little children on there also.
Even some people were afraid.
They didn't know what's gonna happen to them.
You know.
They-- they went on there, and they were brought to the camp, you know, by truck.
And at the very beginning, when we got to Bergen-Belsen, what they did, we were standing there on role call to check out whether nobody had jumped off the train and all that.
And they had these watchtowers where they used to fire-- they kept on firing in the air.
I think it was to scare us.
We-- I didn't know it was-- nobody knew they were firing into the air, but they were, you know, to make a lot of noise shooting.
Nobody was shot.
It was just a scare tactic.

And then we were put up in barracks, and these were completely different from Westerbork, you know, because the little food we had with us, of course, we had ate-- we consumed most of it during the-- during the trip.
We did-- we got nothing during the trip except some water, and then when we got to Westerbork, they-- they sort of gave us-- gave us-- gave you [INAUDIBLE].
Not to-- to Bergen-Belsen, I'm sorry.
They gave you a slice of bread and some-- some soup, some-- some, which was like, you know, turnip soup, whatever it was, watery soup.
That was the food we got when we arrived.
And you know, the men and women were separated.
I was separated from my sister at first.
And it was-- you know, it was sort of-- it was very scary because we didn't know what was going to happen.
And we knew that the only people who were watching us now were the Germans, were the SS, nobody else, OK.
Because in Westerbork, you saw very few Germans inside the camp usually.
Only on the days of the trains, but otherwise you didn't.
Then I noticed there were some young children together in the-- in her same-- in the same barracks as she was, you know, like me, young-- young boys, so I wanted to be moved to be together with her.
And she wanted me to be together with her.
And after a while, we succeeded.
You know, they-- they agreed that I could stay with her, you know, with-- which was a barracks of women, actually.
OK.
And for the first few weeks, except for the lack of food and the lack of sanitation-- this really was the-- the worst part.
Each barrack had sort of a washroom attached to it.
Was a tap.
There was no showers.
You couldn't take a shower.
There was a wash room with a few taps and cold water, which wasn't always there, and you had to go-- if you wanted to go to the toilet, you had to go to, like, an outhouse, further away.
That's all there was.
You know, that's the sanitary conditions we had in Bergen-Belsen at the beginning, but it wasn't too crowded at first.
You know.
When we got there, the only other people in our city-- the camp was divided into several sections.
In our section was a new section, which originally was called the-- the-- it was called a [GERMAN], which means the-- the camp with the stars because we were wearing the Jewish star.
And we were wearing our own clothes.
The rest of the camp were wearing those striped pajamas.
We were not.
We were wearing our own clothes.
And originally, this-- this was considered to be an exchange camp, like I said.
And-- I said to the-- it wasn't very crowded at first.
The only other people were some Greeks from Salonika, who ran the place, who, by the way, were very corrupt, unfortunately.
OK.
They were Jewish, but they were in charge.
And the little food we got, they stole a lot of it.
By the time we got it, we-- there was very little left, you know, especially the bread.
Bread becomes very-- becomes very important in-- in those circumstances.
We got very little of that.
